MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=1] A(1): The paper presents a new corpus and a hand-designed linear readability formula but reports no comparisons against any baselines (no classical readability indices, no prior Vietnamese methods, no machine-learning classifiers), so there is effectively no meaningful empirical comparison. Because all evaluation is reported only on the new corpus with accuracy metrics and Fleiss' kappa but without alternative-method results or head-to-head analyses, fairness and relative performance claims cannot be assessed. Although related work coverage is said to be adequate, the manuscript fails to benchmark its proposal against relevant competing approaches or to acknowledge when prior methods might perform comparably, which is a major omission from a methodological standpoint. | B(1): The paper does not compare against any baselines despite proposing a new readability formula and a new corpus — there are no head-to-head evaluations with prior Vietnamese or multilingual readability methods. While related work coverage is described as adequate, the manuscript fails to run fair comparisons (same data/metrics) or acknowledge when existing methods might perform comparably, nor does it analyze differences to competing approaches. From an applied-resources perspective this omission is critical: claiming ~81% accuracy without baseline benchmarks prevents assessing real utility and progress.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper presents a new corpus and a hand-designed linear readability formula but reports no comparisons against any baselines (no classical readability indices, no prior Vietnamese methods, no machine-learning classifiers), so there is effectively no meaningful empirical comparison. Because all evaluation is reported only on the new corpus with accuracy metrics and Fleiss' kappa but without alternative-method results or head-to-head analyses, fairness and relative performance claims cannot be assessed. Although related work coverage is said to be adequate, the manuscript fails to benchmark its proposal against relevant competing approaches or to acknowledge when prior methods might perform comparably, which is a major omission from a methodological standpoint.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper does not compare against any baselines despite proposing a new readability formula and a new corpus — there are no head-to-head evaluations with prior Vietnamese or multilingual readability methods. While related work coverage is described as adequate, the manuscript fails to run fair comparisons (same data/metrics) or acknowledge when existing methods might perform comparably, nor does it analyze differences to competing approaches. From an applied-resources perspective this omission is critical: claiming ~81% accuracy without baseline benchmarks prevents assessing real utility and progress.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper is generally well organized with a clear intro→method→experiments→conclusion flow and explicitly states the new corpus and proposed readability formula early. Notation is simple and consistent given the linear-formula approach, and writing quality is adequate with related work coverage noted as sufficient. However, presentation suffers minor clarity issues: experimental reporting omits comparisons/baselines and some evaluation details (e.g., hyperparameters/code availability) are not provided, which reduces transparency even if not strictly a readability problem. | B(4): Overall the paper reads clearly and follows a logical structure (introduction, method, experiments, conclusion) and states its main contributions early (new Vietnamese corpus and a simple linear readability formula). Notation and feature definitions are straightforward and seem consistent given the simple linear model and feature list, and the writing quality is adequate with no major grammatical issues. Figures/tables/examples are not described in detail in the summary but presentation appears serviceable; a minor improvement would be clearer presentation of feature correlations and release information (code/data) to strengthen reproducibility.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper is generally well organized with a clear intro→method→experiments→conclusion flow and explicitly states the new corpus and proposed readability formula early. Notation is simple and consistent given the linear-formula approach, and writing quality is adequate with related work coverage noted as sufficient. However, presentation suffers minor clarity issues: experimental reporting omits comparisons/baselines and some evaluation details (e.g., hyperparameters/code availability) are not provided, which reduces transparency even if not strictly a readability problem.

reviewer_b.justification: Overall the paper reads clearly and follows a logical structure (introduction, method, experiments, conclusion) and states its main contributions early (new Vietnamese corpus and a simple linear readability formula). Notation and feature definitions are straightforward and seem consistent given the simple linear model and feature list, and the writing quality is adequate with no major grammatical issues. Figures/tables/examples are not described in detail in the summary but presentation appears serviceable; a minor improvement would be clearer presentation of feature correlations and release information (code/data) to strengthen reproducibility.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper clearly states its claims and provides experimental results on a newly built Vietnamese corpus, but the technical support is incomplete: the proposed linear formula is presented without theoretical justification and is not compared against standard baselines (classical readability indices or learned classifiers). Experimental methodology lacks reported statistical validation (confidence intervals or significance tests) and hyperparameter/control comparisons; Fleiss’ kappa is mentioned but the IAA value and annotation protocol details are not provided in the summary. Given these methodological gaps and the reliance on an ad‑hoc scoring function, the results are suggestive but leave important correctness and robustness questions open. | B(3): The paper makes clear empirical claims (new corpus and a linear readability formula with reported ~81% accuracy) and provides some supporting experiments (including Fleiss' kappa), but the experimental methodology is incomplete: no baselines or statistical significance testing are reported and code/dataset release is not mentioned. The mathematical part is simple and likely correct (a linear formula from interpretable features), however evaluation gaps (coarse labels, missing comparisons to standard classifiers or existing formulas) create uncertainty about how much the formula truly advances practice. Conclusions are plausible given the reported results but are not fully substantiated due to the methodological omissions noted above.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper clearly states its claims and provides experimental results on a newly built Vietnamese corpus, but the technical support is incomplete: the proposed linear formula is presented without theoretical justification and is not compared against standard baselines (classical readability indices or learned classifiers). Experimental methodology lacks reported statistical validation (confidence intervals or significance tests) and hyperparameter/control comparisons; Fleiss’ kappa is mentioned but the IAA value and annotation protocol details are not provided in the summary. Given these methodological gaps and the reliance on an ad‑hoc scoring function, the results are suggestive but leave important correctness and robustness questions open.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper makes clear empirical claims (new corpus and a linear readability formula with reported ~81% accuracy) and provides some supporting experiments (including Fleiss' kappa), but the experimental methodology is incomplete: no baselines or statistical significance testing are reported and code/dataset release is not mentioned. The mathematical part is simple and likely correct (a linear formula from interpretable features), however evaluation gaps (coarse labels, missing comparisons to standard classifiers or existing formulas) create uncertainty about how much the formula truly advances practice. Conclusions are plausible given the reported results but are not fully substantiated due to the methodological omissions noted above.

SUBSTANCE.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper contributes a new Vietnamese readability corpus and an interpretable linear formula but that the experimental support is incomplete: key comparisons to baselines, ablation/sensitivity analyses, reproducibility details (code/hyperparameters), and statistical rigor are missing. Given these substantive gaps that undermine confidence in the claims, a low-but-not-minimal score is warranted to reflect the useful contribution but insufficient empirical validation.

reviewer_a.justification: The experiments are shallow relative to the claims: the paper presents a hand-designed linear readability formula evaluated only on its newly built corpus with accuracy numbers but no baseline comparisons (e.g., adapted classical indices or learned classifiers), no statistical significance tests or confidence intervals, and no reported hyperparameters or code for reproduction. Ablation/sensitivity analysis appears lacking beyond noting low correlation for certain frequency lists; there is no systematic ablation of features or comparison to alternative modelling choices. Related work is described as adequate, and some limitations are acknowledged (coarse difficulty levels, unsuitability of some frequency lists), but key reproducibility details and rigorous evaluation statistics/IAA reporting are missing, undermining the evidential support for the claims.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper provides a useful new Vietnamese readability corpus and a simple interpretable linear formula with reported accuracies (~76–85% per class), which supports the core claim. However, experiments are missing important comparisons and analyses: no baseline models are reported, there are no ablation or sensitivity studies on feature choices, and reproducibility is weak (code/hyperparameters not mentioned). Related work coverage is described as adequate and the authors do discuss limitations of their frequent-word lists and corpus granularity, but overall the empirical evaluation is adequate yet incomplete for stronger confidence.

IMPACT.justification: Both reviewers agree the new Vietnamese corpus and simple, interpretable formula are useful practical contributions, but impact is limited by the ad-hoc nature of the model, coarse labels, lack of comparisons to baselines or richer linguistic features, and missing statistical validation or code release. Given its clear utility for a low-resource language and likelihood to be built upon, yet lack of rigor and novelty to change practice, a moderate impact score is appropriate.

reviewer_a.justification: While building a new Vietnamese readability corpus is a useful engineering contribution, the paper’s impact is limited: the core proposal is an ad‑hoc linear formula based on simple surface features without theoretical grounding or comparison to standard baselines or learned classifiers. Evaluation reports accuracy and Fleiss’ kappa, but lacks rigorous statistical validation (e.g., cross‑validation/confidence intervals) and no comparison to existing readability indices or machine‑learning approaches. Given coarse difficulty classes and admitted omission of deeper linguistic features, the work is a niche, incremental contribution rather than one likely to change practice or open major new directions.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper contributes a useful practical resource and tool for a low-resource language: a newly built Vietnamese readability corpus plus a simple, interpretable linear formula based on length and ‘‘difficult’’ word/syllable features that achieves ~81% accuracy. As an applied researcher I value the corpus release and the formula’s deployability for educational/accessibility applications, so this will be of practical use to the community. However, absence of comparisons to baselines, limited feature depth, coarse difficulty labels, and no mention of released code reduce its immediate influence on research directions. Overall it is a solid, moderate-impact contribution likely to be built upon but not transformative.

ORIGINALITY.justification: Both reviewers agree the main contribution is the new, larger Vietnamese corpus and a simple, interpretable formula, which is practically useful for a low-resource language but methodologically incremental. Given the lack of theoretical justification, limited validation, and absence of comparisons to established baselines or learned models, the work is not highly original but still offers a modest, community‑valuable resource and applied contribution, meriting a middle score.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper mainly assembles known ingredients (surface features like sentence/word length and percent difficult words) into an ad‑hoc linear formula and a newly built corpus; creating a larger Vietnamese corpus is useful but not a methodological novelty. There is no theoretical justification for the formula, no comparison to established baselines or learned classifiers, and no rigorous validation beyond reporting accuracy on the single corpus. The contribution is therefore an incremental, application‑level combination of existing techniques rather than a non‑trivial or surprising advance to readability modelling.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper’s primary novelty is the creation of a new, larger Vietnamese readability corpus and a simple interpretable formula tailored to Vietnamese—contributions that matter practically for low-resource language work. However, the methodological side (linear combination of sentence/word length and % difficult words/syllables) is a fairly incremental application of well-known readability features and lacks comparison to existing baselines or more advanced techniques. From my applied-resources perspective the dataset release plus an easy-to-deploy formula is a notable, community-useful step, but it does not present a highly original modeling advance.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper’s main strength is the new, larger Vietnamese readability corpus but criticize the limited methodological novelty (a simple linear formula), lack of baseline comparisons, weak evaluation rigor, and missing reproducibility artifacts. Given these shared concerns, the submission is a useful applied contribution but falls short of the empirical and methodological standards expected for top-tier venues, so a mid-level score of 3 is appropriate.

reviewer_a.justification: While Vietnamese readability assessment is an NLP topic, the submission is primarily an engineering dataset plus an ad-hoc linear scoring formula without theoretical grounding or comparison to standard baselines (e.g., classical readability indices or learned classifiers). The evaluation lacks rigorous statistical validation (no cross-validation, confidence intervals or significance tests reported) and omits baseline comparisons, which my methodology demands. Although Fleiss’ kappa was reported for annotation, the paper’s limited modeling novelty and weak evaluation rigor make it a better fit for a more applied or language-specific venue than a top-tier NLP/ML conference.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper addresses an applied NLP problem (Vietnamese readability) and contributes a new, larger corpus — a valuable resource that fits the interests of the community I represent. However, the methodological contribution is a simple linear formula with no comparisons to baselines or modern classifiers, code release is not mentioned, and the corpus has coarse labels; these limitations weaken its fit for a top-tier NLP/ML venue that expects stronger empirical baselines, broader evaluation, and reproducibility. Overall it is a useful applied contribution but borderline for top-tier acceptance without additional experiments, baselines, or released artifacts.

main_claims: Created a new, larger corpus for Vietnamese text readability assessment.
Proposed a new formula to predict Vietnamese text readability using features such as word length, sentence length, and percentage of difficult words/syllables.
The proposed formula predicts Vietnamese text readability with overall accuracy up to about 81% (over 76% for easy, over 85% for normal, over 78% for difficult texts).
_summary.methodology: Built a new Vietnamese readability corpus, extracted features (average sentence length in words/syllables/characters, average word length in syllables/characters, percentage of difficult words/syllables using two frequent-word/syllable lists), and derived a linear formula to predict text readability evaluated on the corpus.
datasets: built corpus (newly created Vietnamese readability corpus)
limitations: Percentage of difficult syllables (PDS1) and words (PDW1) computed from Dien and Hao's lists are low correlated with text readability on the built corpus, indicating those frequent lists (from newspaper/normal texts) may not be suitable replacements for easy word/syllable lists.
Current corpus has coarse difficulty levels; future work plans to build corpora with more detailed difficulty levels and domain-specific corpora.
Deeper features (part-of-speech, sentence structure, discourse) and machine learning classifiers were not used and are left for future work.

reviewer_a.name: Theory-first Methodologist
reviewer_a.background: Senior NLP researcher with a PhD in computational linguistics and a track record of work on formal models of language complexity and evaluation methodology.
traits: Penalises papers that present only an empirical/engineering solution without a clear theoretical justification or novelty in modelling (subtract 1-2 points on originality if no theory).
Requires rigorous evaluation statistics: expects cross-validation, confidence intervals or significance tests for reported accuracies (downranks papers lacking them).

Penalises use of ad-hoc linear formulas or hand-designed scoring functions if not compared quantitatively to standard baselines (e.g., classical readability indices adapted to the language, and learned classifiers); deducts points when only self-derived formula is shown.
Demands transparent annotation methodology with reported inter-annotator agreement (IAA); low or unreported IAA reduces credibility and score.
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: Critically examines conceptual contribution and evaluation rigor, giving low scores to papers that are mainly empirical dataset/modeling without theoretical grounding or rigorous validation.

reviewer_b.name: Applied Resources Advocate
reviewer_b.background: Senior applied NLP researcher focused on low-resource languages and creation of datasets and interpretable tools for educational and accessibility applications.
traits: Rewards creation and release of new high-quality language resources (adds 1-2 points for sizable, well-documented corpora with potential community impact).
Values practical, interpretable models and simple formulas that are easy to deploy in real-world systems (gives positive credit for simplicity and interpretability).
Forgives absence of deep theoretical novelty if the work demonstrates clear empirical improvement and utility over prior resources/methods (does not penalise lack of theory).
Rewards reproducibility: expects datasets and code to be released and will boost score if provided (neutral to slightly negative if not).
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: Pragmatic and community-oriented: favors papers that deliver useful datasets, interpretable methods, and clear empirical improvements even if theoretical novelty is limited.
